function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
As of March 31, 2026, SpaceX disclosed approximately 9,600 broadband satellites supporting 10.3M Starlink subscribers and approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites supporting 7.4M monthly unique satellite-to-mobile devices. Calculate the users-per-satellite ratio for each network and explain the utilization implications.

steps.reasoning_content:
Let me break down the question:

1. I need to find the disclosed numbers for Starlink broadband satellites and Starlink subscribers, as well as V1 Mobile satellites and monthly unique satellite-to-mobile devices.

2. Calculate users-per-satellite ratio for each network:
   - Starlink broadband: 10.3M subscribers / 9,600 satellites
   - Mobile: 7.4M monthly unique devices / 650 satellites

Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing and then retrieving the relevant information.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve the specific information about Starlink satellites and subscribers.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve the disclosure about the number of broadband satellites, Starlink subscribers, V1 Mobile satellites, and monthly unique satellite-to-mobile devices as of March 31, 2026 from {{spacex_s1}}.

results.content:
As of March 31, 2026, the preamble’s Starlink overview discloses the following key metrics:

- **Starlink broadband and mobile satellites:** approximately 9,600 operating in Low‑Earth Orbit. (The dedicated satellite‑to‑mobile constellation consisted of *approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites*; a separate section of the preamble notes that the consumer broadband service alone is enabled by *approximately 9,000 broadband satellites*.)
- **Starlink Subscribers:** approximately 10.3 million across 164 countries, territories, and other markets.
- **V1 Mobile satellites:** approximately 650.
- **Monthly unique satellite‑to‑mobile devices:** approximately 7.4 million across approximately 30 countries.

These figures appear in the following consecutive passages from the preamble:

> “communities worldwide. As of March 31, 2026, we had approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in Low-Earth Orbit, providing broadband connectivity to approximately 10.3 million Starlink Subscribers across 164 countries, territories, and other markets. We also provide satellite-to-mobile texting and over-the-top voice services to approximately 7.4 million monthly unique devices across approximately 30 countries.”  
> …  
> “In January 2024, we also began deploying our Starlink Mobile constellation that utilizes separate Starlink satellites with satellite-to-mobile capabilities, substantially reducing mobile “dead zones” around the world. As of March 31, 2026, our dedicated satellite-to-mobile constellation of approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites provides satellite-to-mobile data, over-the-top voice, and messaging services to approximately 7.4 million monthly unique devices across approximately 30 countries.”

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the numbers:

- Starlink broadband: ~9,600 broadband and mobile satellites (with ~9,000 for consumer broadband specifically, and ~650 for mobile)
- Starlink Subscribers: ~10.3 million
- V1 Mobile satellites: ~650
- Monthly unique satellite-to-mobile devices: ~7.4 million

Now for the ratios:

1. Starlink broadband: 10.3M subscribers / 9,600 satellites

But wait, the filing says "approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites." So the 9,600 includes both broadband and mobile satellites. To be precise, we'd need to separate them. However, the question frames it as "approximately 9,600 broadband satellites supporting 10.3M Starlink subscribers" and "approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites supporting 7.4M monthly unique satellite-to-mobile devices."

Let me re-read: "As of March 31, 2026, we had approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in Low-Earth Orbit, providing broadband connectivity to approximately 10.3 million Starlink Subscribers"

So the 9,600 includes both broadband and mobile satellites, and they're attributing the 10.3M subscribers to those 9,600 satellites. But also, separately: "approximately 9,000 broadband satellites" for consumer broadband.

The question states: "approximately 9,600 broadband satellites supporting 10.3M Starlink subscribers." So for the broadband ratio, we use 10.3M / 9,600. 

But actually, if 9,600 includes both broadband and mobile, and the broadband-only satellites are ~9,000, then using 9,600 for the broadband ratio may overcount satellites. But the question specifically says "9,600 broadband satellites" so let me go with that framing.

Let me compute:

1. Broadband: 10,300,000 / 9,600 = let me calculate
2. Mobile: 7,400,000 / 650 = let me calculate

Let me also think about the utilization implications. For broadband, each satellite serves ~1,073 subscribers. For mobile, each satellite serves ~11,385 monthly unique devices. The mobile network has much higher device-to-satellite ratio, which makes sense because satellite-to-mobile is primarily texting/light data services that are intermittent, whereas broadband provides continuous high-bandwidth connectivity.

Let me also think about whether the broadband satellites number should be 9,000 rather than 9,600. The filing says "approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites" and then separately says "consumer broadband service alone is enabled by approximately 9,000 broadband satellites." The question specifically says "approximately 9,600 broadband satellites" - I'll use 9,600 as the question frames it.

Now let me compute these ratios.

Now let me also retrieve some additional context about the nature of these services to better explain the utilization implications. Let me also check if there's relevant information about capacity or bandwidth to enrich the analysis.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve any disclosures about Starlink satellite capacity, bandwidth per satellite, or network utilization from {{spacex_s1}}.
Retrieve disclosures about the difference between Starlink broadband service and satellite-to-mobile service, including bandwidth and use case differences from {{spacex_s1}}.

results.content:
The provided text contains the following disclosures regarding Starlink satellite capacity, bandwidth per satellite, and indications of network utilization:

**Cumulative constellation capacity**
- “Our current constellations of approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites, including over 3,000 satellites deployed in 2025, support **over 700 Tbps of cumulative downlink capacity**.”

**Per‑satellite bandwidth (V3 satellites)**
- The next‑generation V3 satellites are “designed to offer **one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite**.”  
- The same 1 Tbps per‑satellite figure is repeated in multiple sections, including the definition of “V3 satellites”: “refers to our next‑generation Starlink broadband satellites, which are designed to offer **one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite**.”

**Launch‑scaled capacity deployment**
- “We expect that a single Starship launch will be capable of deploying up to 60 V3 satellites to LEO, representing a potential **twenty‑fold increase in Starlink downlink capacity deployed relative to a Falcon 9 launch**.”

**Indirect utilization metric (peak‑hour performance)**
- While not a direct network utilization percentage, the document provides a service performance figure that reflects network load: “We provide fiber‑like download speeds—at a **median of 225 Mbps during peak hours** for residential users as of March 31, 2026.”

No explicit network utilization rate (e.g., percentage of capacity in use) is disclosed.

The prospectus clearly distinguishes Starlink’s consumer broadband service from its satellite-to-mobile offering. Below are the key disclosures regarding their differences, including intended use cases, performance specifications, and technical evolution.

### Fundamental Design Difference
The two systems are explicitly separated by purpose and architecture:
> “Our broadband satellites are designed to deliver high-speed internet services to homes, businesses, and vehicles, while our mobile satellites are designed to connect directly to cell phones from space. These constellations have different performance requirements and technical specifications.”  
*(Prospectus Summary, glossary of satellite terms)*

### Current Service Capabilities and Bandwidth

**Starlink Consumer Broadband**  
- Provides “fiber-like download speeds—at a median of 225 Mbps during peak hours for residential users as of March 31, 2026.”  
- Median latency is “approximately 25 milliseconds,” low enough for “intensive real-time applications, such as content streaming, video calls, and online gaming.”  
- Enterprise and mobility solutions (aviation, maritime) can deliver “download speeds exceeding 400 Mbps with round-trip latencies as low as 21 milliseconds.”  

**Satellite-to-Mobile (Starlink Mobile)**  
- The current V1 Mobile satellites offer **light data, text messaging (SMS), and over-the-top voice services** (e.g., WhatsApp, FaceTime) directly to standard, unmodified LTE handsets.  
- “We also provide satellite-to-mobile texting and over-the-top voice services to approximately 7.4 million monthly unique devices across approximately 30 countries.”  
- No broadband data speeds are quoted for the current mobile service—it is explicitly a “light data” and messaging service, designed to “substantially reduc[e] mobile dead zones.”

### Planned Evolution and Future Bandwidth
- **V3 Broadband Satellites:** Next-generation satellites “designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite,” deployable via Starship, with a single launch delivering up to 60 satellites and a “twenty-fold increase in Starlink downlink capacity” compared to a Falcon 9 launch.  
- **V2 Mobile Satellites:** The next-generation mobile satellites “are designed to provide more comprehensive satellite-to-mobile services, including **broadband data and IoT connectivity**.” Together with the EchoStar spectrum acquisition and optimized 5G protocols, these satellites “are expected to increase capacity by orders of magnitude compared to our first-generation constellation.”

### Use Case Differences
- **Broadband use cases:** Fixed residential, small/medium business, enterprise (construction, agriculture, retail, hospitality, aviation, maritime, land mobility), and government. The service requires a Starlink user terminal (Standard, Mini, or Performance) but can support in-motion connectivity up to 100 mph and global oceanwide coverage.  
- **Mobile use cases:** Direct-to-smartphone connectivity with no additional hardware; eliminating mobile dead zones; emergency communications during natural disasters; supplemental coverage for mobile network operators (MNOs) in remote areas. The mobile service is positioned as an extension of terrestrial cellular networks, not a replacement for fixed broadband.

In summary, Starlink Broadband is a high-throughput, low-latency internet service for homes and enterprises using dedicated terminals, while Starlink Mobile is a direct-to-phone service currently offering narrowband messaging and voice, with a roadmap toward broadband data and IoT through future satellite generations.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me also think about the nuance in the satellite count. The question says "approximately 9,600 broadband satellites" but the filing says "approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites." So the 9,600 figure includes both broadband and mobile satellites.

The filing also says the consumer broadband service is enabled by "approximately 9,000 broadband satellites." So if we wanted to be more precise about the broadband-only count, it would be ~9,000. But the question frames it as 9,600 broadband satellites for the broadband ratio.

Let me note this nuance in my answer. The question specifically says "approximately 9,600 broadband satellites" so I'll compute using that number, but I should note that this figure actually includes both broadband and mobile satellites per the filing.

Now let me compute:

Starlink Broadband: 10,300,000 / 9,600 = 1,072.92 subscribers per satellite
Starlink Mobile: 7,400,000 / 650 = 11,384.62 monthly unique devices per satellite

Now for the utilization implications:

1. The mobile ratio (~11,385 devices/satellite) is about 10.6x higher than the broadband ratio (~1,073 subscribers/satellite).

2. This makes sense because:
   - Broadband: high-bandwidth, continuous connectivity, median 225 Mbps during peak hours, supports streaming, gaming, video calls. Each subscriber consumes significant capacity continuously.
   - Mobile: light data, SMS, OTT voice - intermittent, low-bandwidth usage. Devices connect only sporadically, not continuously.
   
3. The cumulative downlink capacity is over 700 Tbps across the ~9,600 satellites. At 1,073 subscribers per satellite, that's roughly 700 Tbps / 10.3M = ~68 Mbps per subscriber on average (but this is just rough, since satellites aren't evenly distributed).

4. The mobile network has much higher device density per satellite because each device uses minimal bandwidth and connects intermittently - the satellites are essentially providing coverage (eliminating dead zones) rather than sustained high-throughput connections.

Let me also note the distinction between "subscribers" (broadband, paying customers with terminals) and "monthly unique devices" (mobile, any device that connected during the month, potentially free/non-paying users in some cases).

Let me also compute the ratio difference more precisely.

10,384.62 / 1,072.92 ≈ 10.61x

Now, let me also think about whether I should use 9,000 instead of 9,600 for the broadband ratio. The question says "approximately 9,600 broadband satellites." Let me calculate both and note the difference.

If using 9,000: 10,300,000 / 9,000 = 1,144.44

But the question explicitly states 9,600, so I'll use that.

Let me now craft my final answer.
The mobile ratio is about 10.6x the broadband ratio. Let me now compose my final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Users-per-Satellite Ratios (as of March 31, 2026)

### Starlink Broadband Network

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Broadband satellites (incl. mobile) | ~9,600 |
| Starlink Subscribers | ~10.3M |
| **Subscribers per satellite** | **~1,073** |

**Calculation:** 10,300,000 ÷ 9,600 = **1,072.92 subscribers per satellite**

*Nuance:* The S-1 discloses ~9,600 as "Starlink broadband and mobile satellites" combined; a separate passage notes that consumer broadband alone is enabled by "approximately 9,000 broadband satellites." Using 9,000 would yield ~1,144 subscribers per satellite. The question's framing of "9,600 broadband satellites" is used for this calculation consistent with the instructions.

---

### Starlink Mobile (V1) Network

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| V1 Mobile satellites | ~650 |
| Monthly unique satellite-to-mobile devices | ~7.4M |
| **Monthly unique devices per satellite** | **~11,385** |

**Calculation:** 7,400,000 ÷ 650 = **11,384.62 monthly unique devices per satellite**

---

### Ratio Comparison

The mobile network carries approximately **10.6×** more users per satellite than the broadband network (11,385 vs. 1,073).

---

## Utilization Implications

### 1. Fundamentally Different Service Profiles

The 10.6× disparity is not a signal of relative efficiency or underutilization—it reflects two entirely different network architectures serving qualitatively different use cases:

- **Starlink Broadband** delivers high-throughput, continuous connectivity: median **225 Mbps during peak hours**, ~25 ms latency, supporting streaming, video calls, and online gaming. Each subscriber's user terminal maintains a persistent, bandwidth-intensive connection. The constellation's cumulative downlink capacity exceeds **700 Tbps**, implying ~68 Mbps of average capacity per subscriber across the footprint (though capacity is not evenly distributed geographically).

- **Starlink Mobile (V1)** provides narrowband services: **SMS texting, light data, and over-the-top voice** (WhatsApp, FaceTime) directly to unmodified LTE handsets. These are inherently intermittent, low-bandwidth interactions—a device may connect only for seconds to transmit a text message. No broadband data speeds are quoted for V1 Mobile service.

### 2. Coverage vs. Throughput Paradigms

The broadband network operates on a **throughput-constrained** model: each satellite's finite downlink capacity must be shared among concurrent subscribers, and adding subscribers without adding capacity degrades service. The ~1,073 subscribers-per-satellite ratio reflects an equilibrium where SpaceX can maintain its advertised median 225 Mbps peak-hour performance.

The mobile network operates on a **coverage-constrained** model: the primary value proposition is eliminating "dead zones" by providing any connectivity at all. Because each device consumes minimal airtime, a single V1 Mobile satellite can serve thousands of unique devices per month without congestion. The ~11,385 devices-per-satellite ratio measures reach and coverage density, not capacity utilization.

### 3. Distinction Between "Subscribers" and "Monthly Unique Devices"

The two metrics are not directly comparable in economic terms. "Starlink Subscribers" are paying customers with dedicated hardware terminals. "Monthly unique devices" count any handset that connected to the mobile network during the month—potentially including non-paying users (e.g., emergency connectivity, free-tier service through MNO partnerships). This further explains the higher device-per-satellite figure on the mobile side.

### 4. Growth Trajectory and Capacity Expansion

Both networks are scaling. Over 3,000 satellites were deployed in 2025 alone. The next-generation **V3 broadband satellites** are designed for **1 Tbps downlink capacity per satellite** (vs. the current fleet average of ~73 Mbps per satellite, based on 700 Tbps ÷ 9,600). A single Starship launch can deploy up to 60 V3 satellites—a potential 20× increase in downlink capacity per launch versus Falcon 9. On the mobile side, **V2 Mobile satellites** are designed to add broadband data and IoT connectivity, increasing capacity "by orders of magnitude."

### 5. Key Analytical Takeaway

The users-per-satellite ratios for the two networks should not be compared as if they measure the same thing. The broadband ratio (~1,073) reflects a capacity-sharing constraint for a high-bandwidth service; the mobile ratio (~11,385) reflects coverage reach for a narrowband service. The mobile network can sustain far higher device counts per satellite precisely because per-device data consumption is orders of magnitude lower. As V2 Mobile satellites introduce broadband data, the mobile users-per-satellite ratio would be expected to compress toward levels more analogous to the broadband network.

| Starlink Subscribers | ~10.3M |
| **Subscribers per satellite** | **~1,073** |

**Calculation:** 10,300,000 ÷ 9,600 = **1,072.92 subscribers per satellite**

---

| **Monthly unique devices per satellite** | **~11,385** |

---

The mobile network carries approximately **10.6×** more users per satellite than the broadband network (11,385 vs. 1,073).

---

### 4. Growth Trajectory and Capacity Expansion

Both networks are scaling. Over 3,000 satellites were deployed in 2025 alone. The next-generation **V3 broadband satellites** are designed for **1 Tbps downlink capacity per satellite** (vs. the current fleet average of ~73 Mbps per satellite, based on 700 Tbps ÷ 9,600). A single Starship launch can deploy up to 60 V3 satellites—a potential 20× increase in downlink capacity per launch versus Falcon 9. On the mobile side, **V2 Mobile satellites** are designed to add broadband data and IoT connectivity, increasing capacity "by orders of magnitude."

### 5. Key Analytical Takeaway

The users-per-satellite ratios for the two networks should not be compared as if they measure the same thing. The broadband ratio (~1,073) reflects a capacity-sharing constraint for a high-bandwidth service; the mobile ratio (~11,385) reflects coverage reach for a narrowband service. The mobile network can sustain far higher device counts per satellite precisely because per-device data consumption is orders of magnitude lower. As V2 Mobile satellites introduce broadband data, the mobile users-per-satellite ratio would be expected to compress toward levels more analogous to the broadband network.
